Oh my stars, honey, I'm not sure I understand. Could you say that again a little slower? I don't hear so well since my operation.
Now hold on just a moment, dear — Mr. Whiskers is sitting right on my telephone table and I can barely reach the receiver. Where were we?
The IRS, you say? My goodness. Let me find my reading glasses so I can write this down properly. I know I set them right here somewhere...
Oh, I do worry about these things. My son-in-law Gerald said I should never give out my Social Security number on the phone, but he worries about everything, bless his heart.
I'm sorry, could you repeat that number? I had to put the phone down for a second because the cookies in the oven were starting to smell a little funny. Now — what was it you needed?
Arrested? Oh my goodness gracious. I certainly don't want any trouble. Let me just — hold on, Mr. Whiskers, not now — let me find my checkbook.
Gift cards? Well now, that does seem a little unusual. My grandson Bobby says the IRS never asks for gift cards. He's very smart about computers. Should I call him?
I'm so sorry, I got a little turned around there. Could you start from the beginning? I want to make sure I understand everything correctly, honey.
Well, I have my purse right here somewhere... I set it down when I was making my pecan pie this morning. You should smell it, dear, the whole house smells wonderful.
Now, how much did you say I owe? Because I have my savings account and my Christmas club account, and I want to make sure I'm looking at the right thing.
I appreciate your patience, I really do. At my age these things take a little longer. My doctor says I need to slow down, and I'm trying my best.
Oh, I hear you, I do. But Mr. Whiskers just knocked something off the shelf and I need to check — just one moment, sweetheart — okay, I'm back. What was the question?
You know, I used to be a schoolteacher, and I always taught my students to double-check their work. So you don't mind if I just double-check all this with my bank first, do you?
